title: Counterparty Concentration Risk

content.statement: Dependence on a few relationships makes the business more brittle than top-line revenue suggests.
content.question: What percentage of your revenue or users comes from your top 5 customers or partners, and is that concentration rising or falling?
sop: List your top 10 customers, suppliers, or partners by revenue or volume share.
Calculate each one's share of total revenue as a percentage (see calculation, page 6).
Square each share and sum them to get your concentration index (see calculation, page 6).
If your top 1-2 relationships account for more than 40% combined, set a target to bring that below 25% within two quarters.
Add one new customer, supplier, or channel relationship each month until concentration trends down.
content.explainer: Big wins feel like traction and are actually concentration. Track it. Diversify intentionally.
questions: What percentage of your revenue or users comes from your top 5 customers or partners, and is that concentration rising or falling?
If your single biggest customer, supplier, or partner disappeared tomorrow, what percentage of your revenue goes with them?
Are you actively adding new customers or partners, or is your growth just making your biggest relationships bigger?

definitions.def: The other party in a business relationship, a customer, supplier, or partner you depend on.
A standard measure of concentration: square each party's share and add them up. A result near 1 means a few relationships dominate; near 0 means things are spread evenly.

content.equation_example: Share 1 at 30%, Share 2 at 25%, Share 3 at 20%, Share 4 at 15%, Share 5 at 10%. Squared and summed: 0.09 plus 0.0625 plus 0.04 plus 0.0225 plus 0.01 equals 0.225 out of a possible 1.0. Higher, closer to 1, means revenue is concentrated in a few relationships, one loss could sink you. Lower, closer to 0, means revenue is spread evenly and no single relationship can hurt you badly.
content.closing_statement: Diversification is insurance you buy before you need it.

caption:
Principle Nº 48: Counterparty Concentration Risk

Dependence on a few relationships makes the business more brittle than top-line revenue suggests.

Ask yourself: What percentage of your revenue or users comes from your top 5 customers or partners, and is that concentration rising or falling?
